Let me be honest with you — I was skeptical

I'd been paying €180/month for reformer Pilates classes. Two sessions a week, which sounds great until you factor in the commute, the scheduling, and the very real social anxiety of being the least coordinated person in the room.

Then I saw this little folding board all over Pinterest. My first thought? Looks like a gimmick. My second thought, after 12 minutes on it for the first time: my legs were shaking. My core was on fire. I was genuinely sweating.

I've now been using the ExeractFit Foldable Pilates Board daily for 30 days. Here's everything I discovered.

"In 10 sessions you'll feel the difference. In 20 you'll see it. In 30, you'll have a new body." — Joseph Pilates

Why Most Women Never Actually Get to Do Pilates

Here's a number that stuck with me: 65% of women avoid the gym specifically because of fear of judgment. Not because they don't want to work out. Because walking into a studio full of people who already know what they're doing feels absolutely terrifying.

And even if you push past that? Studio reformer Pilates costs €25–50 per class. Go twice a week and that's over €2,600 per year. A full reformer machine for home? €1,000 to €2,000, minimum — and it takes up the space of a dining table.

This is exactly the gap this board fills. And it fills it surprisingly well.

😰

What Actually Comes in the Box

The ExeractFit board ships with everything you need to start immediately — no separate purchases, no searching for compatible accessories. You get the foldable board itself, two latex resistance bands with foam-grip handles (rated to 65 lbs), a built-in timer, a phone holder, a 20mm knee pad, and elbow cushions.

The board supports up to 150 kg (330 lbs) without any flex or deformation — I tested this personally and was impressed at how stable it felt. The reinforced PP construction is noticeably sturdier than cheaper alternatives.

The 2025 upgraded ExeractFit board with all included accessories

7 Exercises, One Board — What a Workout Actually Looks Like

This is the part that genuinely surprised me. I expected maybe two or three exercises I'd get bored of quickly. What you actually get is a full-body training system that keeps progressing as you get stronger.

Six of the many exercises possible — hip training, mountain climbers, core work, balance, resistance bands, and back stretch

My favourite starter routine takes exactly 15 minutes and hits everything: ab rolling for core, hip hinges for glutes, resistance band rows for posture, and mountain climbers for cardio. The automatic rebound mechanism on the ab roller is a game-changer — it controls the return so your core stays engaged the entire movement, rather than you just yanking yourself back up.

The Workout I Do Every Morning

Ab Roller (2 × 10 reps) — Kneel on the included knee pad, grip the handles, roll forward slowly and let the board spring back. Your core will be crying by rep 6. Hip Bridges (3 × 15) — Feet on the board for instability, drives serious glute activation. Resistance Band Rows (3 × 12) — Stand on the board, pull the handles to your hips. Posture improves noticeably within two weeks. Mountain Climbers (2 × 30 seconds) — Hands on push-up handles, drive knees alternately. Cardio and core together.

"I do 20-minute sessions and finish sweating every time. You genuinely feel the work — this thing does not let you cheat."

It Doesn't Matter If You've Never Done Pilates Before — Or If You've Done It For Years

This is one of the things I want to be very clear about, because it changed how I thought about this board: it genuinely works at every skill level. You don't need to know a single Pilates move to get started. And if you're already advanced, you won't outgrow it either.

"I'd never done Pilates once in my life. I just unfolded the board, found a YouTube routine, and within 10 minutes I understood exactly what I was doing. There was no awkward learning curve."

For complete beginners, the board is forgiving by design. The sliding surface guides your movement naturally — your body figures out the right path intuitively, even before your brain has processed the instruction. Exercises like the ab roller have an automatic rebound mechanism that controls your range of motion, so you can't accidentally overextend. The included knee and elbow pads mean you can work on hard floors without discomfort. You simply start slow, do what feels right, and build from there.

For intermediate users, the 7-in-1 system gives you real variety to keep progressing. You rotate between core work, resistance training, balance exercises, and stretching — so you're never stuck repeating the same three movements. The resistance bands provide enough challenge to make you work, and as you get stronger you naturally increase your range, your speed, and the number of reps.

For advanced practitioners, the board introduces instability as a training variable. When you're strong enough to hold a plank perfectly on solid ground, doing it with your hands on a gliding surface recruits entirely new stabiliser muscles. Mountain climbers become dramatically harder. Hip bridges with feet on the board challenge your glutes and hamstrings in ways a standard mat never will. The board doesn't cap your progress — it evolves with you.

🌱

Gym-Quality Workouts From Your Sofa — Without the €50 Price Tag Per Class

Here's something nobody talks about enough: one of the biggest reasons home workouts used to feel inferior to the gym was that you were working out alone, without a trainer, following a piece of paper. That's no longer the reality.

Today, YouTube has hundreds of free, instructor-led Pilates board routines ranging from 10 minutes to 45 minutes, taught by certified instructors who would normally charge €25–50 per session in a studio. You unfold your board in front of your TV or laptop, press play, and follow along — pausing when you need to, rewinding when you miss a cue, never feeling embarrassed for going slower than the person next to you.

"I'm essentially getting a personal trainer session every morning. I press play, follow along, and my living room becomes a studio. The difference is I'm paying zero per session instead of €35."

The ExeractFit board is built for exactly this setup. The built-in phone holder positions your screen at perfect eye level during floor exercises — no more craning your neck to see the TV from a mat, or balancing your phone against a water bottle. The built-in timer means you track your intervals without reaching for your phone and breaking your rhythm. You set up once, press play, and just move.

Popular instructor channels like Callie Gualy (531K followers, certified reformer Pilates instructor) and Move With Nicole have dedicated pilates board workout playlists — beginners through advanced — all completely free. That's the full studio Pilates experience, at home, on your schedule, at zero cost per session. The board pays for itself after a single month of replacing studio classes.

The Storage Problem Is Completely Solved

This was my biggest concern before buying. My apartment is small. I didn't want another piece of equipment that would live in the corner gathering guilt.

It folds to half its size in one second and slides under a sofa, bed, or beside a shelf

The fold takes literally one second. One click, halved in size, slides under my couch. I pull it out before my morning coffee, do my 15 minutes, fold it back, and it's gone. My partner doesn't even know I own it.

This single feature is what makes consistent use actually realistic. Because the biggest predictor of whether you'll use fitness equipment is whether it's in your way or not. This one stays invisible until you need it.

The Honest Limitations (Because You Deserve the Full Picture)

I'm not going to pretend this board is perfect, because it's not. Here's what I'd want to know upfront:

The included manual is nearly useless. It shows a few stick figures that aren't particularly helpful. The fix is easy — YouTube has dozens of excellent beginner pilates board routines (the video above is a great starting point), and honestly discovering workouts online is more fun than following a leaflet anyway.

The resistance bands are good, not great. They're solid for a beginner or intermediate user. If you're already doing serious strength training, you may outgrow them eventually. But for most people using this for core, glutes, and posture work, they're perfectly adequate.

It's reformer-adjacent, not reformer-identical. A €49 board will never completely replicate a €2,000 studio machine. But in my experience, it delivers about 80% of the results at about 2% of the annual cost. That's a trade I'm happy to make permanently.

This Is For You If...

  • 👩‍💻
    You're time-poor but not motivation-poor. You want to work out but a 60-minute class with a 20-minute commute just isn't happening consistently. Fifteen minutes in your living room before work? That's doable every single day.
  • 💰
    You love Pilates but the class prices are burning a hole. If you've done studio reformer classes and you love the results but wince at the invoice, this is exactly what you've been waiting for.
  • 🏠
    You live in a small apartment. No garage, no spare room, no dedicated gym corner. This slides under a bed and appears in your living room in one second flat.
  • 🌱
    You're a complete beginner. You want to try Pilates but the idea of walking into a studio without knowing what you're doing is terrifying. Your living room is the perfect classroom — pause, rewind, no pressure.
  • 💪
    You want visible core and glute results. Not just "feeling healthier" but actually seeing your body change. Consistent use produces visible toning within 2–3 weeks for most people — core first, then legs and glutes.

A Closer Look at the Build Quality

One thing that surprised me about this board is how solid it feels. The reinforced PP construction, the quality of the foam handles, and the smooth action of the ab roller mechanism all feel more premium than the price suggests.

Built-in handles rotate 360°, resistance bands clip in and out, and the anti-slip base is completely silent on hard floors

The 360° rotating handles mean you can work your wrists and forearms through natural movement paths rather than fixed positions. The anti-slip base keeps the board completely stationary on hardwood or tile — no mat needed. And importantly, it's completely silent, which matters if you're working out while someone else is sleeping or in the next room.

The ExeractFit Foldable Pilates Board delivers on its core promise: reformer-quality results in a home workout, at a fraction of the cost, in a fraction of the space. The build is surprisingly sturdy, the 7-in-1 functionality keeps workouts varied, and the 1-second fold is genuinely life-changing for anyone in a small home.

I'm €180/month richer. My core is visibly stronger. My posture is better. And I haven't been to a studio in five weeks — nor do I want to.
